model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Houston is one of the elite teams in the entire tournament, ranking 5th nationally with an adjusted efficiency margin of +33.55 that is roughly 10 points per 100 possessions higher than Saint Mary's +23.11, driven by the nation's 4th-ranked defense (AdjD 91.0) that should severely limit Saint Mary's 43rd-ranked offense. The Gaels face a critical health concern with starting center Harry Wessels dealing with a rolled ankle from the WCC semifinals that limited him to just nine minutes, which would be devastating against Houston's physical, Big 12-tested interior. While Saint Mary's solid 22nd-ranked defense gives them a credible path to keeping the game competitive, the massive quality gap across every dimension—combined with Houston's suffocating defensive identity under Kelvin Sampson and the significant schedule strength disparity between the Big 12 and WCC—makes the Cougars a comfortable favorite to advance.

reasoningStep.title: Houston's elite defense and 10-point efficiency margin advantage should overwhelm Saint Mary's
reasoningStep.summary: Houston (KenPom #5, AdjEM +33.55) holds a commanding ~10-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage over Saint Mary's (KenPom #24, AdjEM +23.11), with the nation's 4th-ranked defense poised to stifle the Gaels' 43rd-ranked offense. Saint Mary's starting center Harry Wessels is dealing with a rolled ankle that limited him to 9 minutes in the WCC semifinals, further weakening their ability to compete inside against Houston's physical roster.
evidence: Houston KenPom #5: AdjO 124.6 (16th), AdjD 91.0 (4th), AdjEM +33.55; 28-5 record in Big 12
Saint Mary's KenPom #24: AdjO 120.3 (43rd), AdjD 97.2 (22nd), AdjEM +23.11; 27-5 record in WCC
Harry Wessels (Saint Mary's starting center) rolled ankle in WCC semifinals warmups, managed only 9 minutes in loss to Santa Clara—status uncertain
Houston's defense is 6+ points per 100 possessions better than Saint Mary's defense (91.0 vs 97.2), creating a massive asymmetry
Big 12 schedule strength vastly exceeds WCC, meaning Houston's metrics are more battle-tested

Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though there

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Barcelona vs. Newcastle Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four 7m ago Consensus Bracket Winning Expectations Expert Picks Tournament Forecast The Perfect Bracket Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though there

It hasn’t been an easy time around the program during this brutal stretch of facing three of the best teams in the country in quick succession. Most of the college basketball world was expecting Houston vs Kansas to be a great game, but ultimately it did not live up to that. As good as Kansas has been at Allen Fieldhouse, Houston has not lost by double digits since 2024. --> It hasn’t been an easy time around the program during this brutal stretch of facing three of the best teams in the country in quick succession. As good as Kansas has been at Allen Fieldhouse, Houston has not lost by double digits since 2024. While there were various reasons why the Cougars did not play well against Kansas, the situation became much more clear during coach Kelvin Sampson’s post-game press conference . Houston had just run out of steam. That was shown in the second half where the Cougars just shot 32% overall. --> While there were various reasons why the Cougars did not play well against Kansas, the situation became much more clear during coach Kelvin Sampson’s post-game press conference . modelTrace.content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me summarize the key data:

**Houston (2-seed):**
- KenPom #5: AdjO 124.6 (16th), AdjD 91.0 (4th), AdjEM +33.55
- Torvik #5: AdjO 125.1 (13th), AdjD 91.9 (4th)
- Record: 28-5, Big 12
- Elite defense, strong offense
- Had a 3-game losing streak mid-season but recovered; some injury concerns with two seniors (from SI article) but no season-ending issues reported

**Saint Mary's (7-seed):**
- KenPom #24: AdjO 120.3 (43rd), AdjD 97.2 (22nd), AdjEM +23.11
- Torvik #25: AdjO 119.4 (48th), AdjD 97.6 (22nd)
- Record: 27-5, WCC
- Key concern: Starting center Harry Wessels rolled his ankle in WCC semifinals warmups, managed only 9 minutes in the loss to Santa Clara. Status uncertain for this game.
- Good defense but significantly lower efficiency margin than Houston

**Key matchup factors:**
- Houston's efficiency margin advantage: +33.55 vs +23.11 = ~10.4 points per 100 possessions
- Houston's defense (AdjD 91.0) is elite and should limit Saint Mary's offense (AdjO 120.3)
- Houston's offense (AdjO 124.6) should exploit Saint Mary's good-but-not-elite defense (AdjD 97.2)
- Saint Mary's potential loss of starting center Wessels is a significant blow against Houston's physical interior
- WCC schedule strength is a concern vs Big 12 battle-testing

```json
{
  "pick": {
    "gameId": "r32-south-4",
    "winnerId": "houston",
    "confidence": 0.9,
    "rationale": "Houston is one of the elite teams in the entire tournament, ranking 5th nationally with an adjusted efficiency margin of +33.55 that is roughly 10 points per 100 possessions higher than Saint Mary's +23.11, driven by the nation's 4th-ranked defense (AdjD 91.0) that should severely limit Saint Mary's 43rd-ranked offense. The Gaels face a critical health concern with starting center Harry Wessels dealing with a rolled ankle from the WCC semifinals that limited him to just nine minutes, which would be devastating against Houston's physical, Big 12-tested interior. While Saint Mary's solid 22nd-ranked defense gives them a credible path to keeping the game competitive, the massive quality gap across every dimension—combined with Houston's suffocating defensive identity under Kelvin Sampson and the significant schedule strength disparity between the Big 12 and WCC—makes the Cougars a comfortable favorite to advance."
  },
  "reasoningStep": {
    "id": "r32-south-4",
    "title": "Houston's elite defense and 10-point efficiency margin advantage should overwhelm Saint Mary's",
    "summary": "Houston (KenPom #5, AdjEM +33.55) holds a commanding ~10-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage over Saint Mary's (KenPom #24, AdjEM +23.11), with the nation's 4th-ranked defense poised to stifle the Gaels' 43rd-ranked offense. Saint Mary's starting center Harry Wessels is dealing with a rolled ankle that limited him to 9 minutes in the WCC semifinals, further weakening their ability to compete inside against Houston's physical roster.",
    "evidence": [
      "Houston KenPom #5: AdjO 124.6 (16th), AdjD 91.0 (4th), AdjEM +33.55; 28-5 record in Big 12",
      "Saint Mary's KenPom #24: AdjO 120.3 (43rd), AdjD 97.2 (22nd), AdjEM +23.11; 27-5 record in WCC",
      "Harry Wessels (Saint Mary's starting center) rolled ankle in WCC semifinals warmups, managed only 9 minutes in loss to Santa Clara—status uncertain",
      "Houston's defense is 6+ points per 100 possessions better than Saint Mary's defense (91.0 vs 97.2), creating a massive asymmetry",
      "Big 12 schedule strength vastly exceeds WCC, meaning Houston's metrics are more battle-tested"
    ]
  }
}
```
